summary: Jeff Atwood shares a collection of Windows registry tweaks he's compiled and enhanced, building on Scott Hanselman's power user tips. The post provides practical modifications including adding command prompt access to folder right-click menus, fixing Windows XP's broken file search that ignores unknown extensions, improving the Disk Cleanup wizard by removing useless options and adding more aggressive cleanup rules, and adding useful shortcuts to the My Computer context menu. Atwood emphasizes these are quality-of-life improvements that should have been Windows defaults, while acknowledging the risks of registry editing and providing rollback files.
key_points: Windows XP's built-in search inexplicably ignores files without known extension associations by default, a design flaw that can waste hours of debugging time
The Disk Cleanup wizard ships with time-wasting features like 'compress old files' that can be fixed through registry modifications
Right-click context menus are highly customizable through the registry, allowing quick access to regedit, services, and Add/Remove Programs from My Computer
The built-in Send To folder provides a simpler alternative to registry hacks for adding items to right-click menus
Registry tweaks require caution but can dramatically improve Windows usability when the defaults are poorly designed
money_quotes: For some godforsaken reason, Start, Search only searches files with known extension associations by default.
I've run into this 'feature' before and I nearly pulled my hair out trying to figure out why it couldn't find the source file that I knew was there.
Nothing refreshes like giving the Windows undercarriage a little how's your father.
Once you try it, you'll wonder why these menu options aren't there to begin with.
This should probably go without saying, but we are deleting stuff here. Use this at your own risk.
key_insight: Windows ships with baffling default behaviors that power users must fix themselves through registry hacks, revealing a gap between Microsoft's design decisions and what developers actually need.
